prompt: You're a journalist and I'm the only witness to a crime. Start your interview.
constraint: Student must ask at least 5 different questions before I'm allowed to leave.
studentPrompt: You are a journalist interviewing the only witness to a crime. Ask as many questions as you can to find out what happened.
studentStarter: Can you tell me what you saw?
Where exactly were you when it happened?
What time did this take place?
studentFollowUp: Have you ever had to ask someone difficult questions in real life?

prompt: I'm new here and completely lost. You're the local who knows everything. Help me.
constraint: Student cannot use the words 'go', 'turn', or 'walk'. Find alternatives.
studentPrompt: A visitor is completely lost in your city. You are the local expert. Help them find their way and make them feel welcome.
studentStarter: So where are you trying to get to?
Okay, so from here you need to...
It's not far — let me explain.
studentFollowUp: Have you ever been lost somewhere and needed help from a stranger?

prompt: You are my new boss. Today is my first performance review.
constraint: Student must give both positive feedback AND one area to improve.
studentPrompt: You are a manager giving a new employee their first performance review. Say something positive and suggest one thing they could improve.
studentStarter: Overall I think you've made a great start...
One thing I'd like to see more of is...
You've been doing really well with...
studentFollowUp: Have you ever received feedback that really helped you improve?

prompt: I've just arrived from 200 years in the past. Explain smartphones to me.
constraint: Student can only use analogies and comparisons — no technical vocabulary.
studentPrompt: Explain what a smartphone is to someone from 200 years in the past. Use comparisons and everyday ideas — no technical words allowed.
studentStarter: Imagine a very thin book that can talk...
Think of it like a messenger, but instant...
It's similar to a letter, except...
studentFollowUp: What invention from the last 50 years do you think is the most important?

prompt: You're the customer. I'm the terrible waiter. Complain to me.
constraint: Student must be politely assertive — no shouting, no insults.
studentPrompt: You are a customer at a restaurant and the waiter has been terrible. Complain politely but firmly — no shouting or insults.
studentPractice: Past tenses to describe what went wrong
studentStarter: Excuse me, I'm afraid there's a problem with...
I've been waiting for quite a while now...
I don't want to cause trouble, but...
studentFollowUp: Have you ever complained about bad service in real life? What happened?

prompt: You're a detective. I'm a suspect. You have 5 minutes to catch me in a lie.
constraint: Student must use at least one tag question per turn (e.g. 'You were there, weren't you?').
studentPrompt: You are a detective and your teacher is a suspect. Try to catch them in a lie. Use tag questions to challenge what they say.
studentStarter: You were there that evening, weren't you?
That doesn't quite add up, does it?
So you're saying you never met them, is that right?
studentFollowUp: Do you enjoy watching detective shows or films? What do you like about them?

prompt: I'm your employee who keeps making the same mistake. Handle this conversation.
constraint: Student must avoid the word 'you' when criticising. Rephrase everything.
studentPrompt: You are a manager who needs to talk to an employee about a repeated mistake. Try to give criticism without using the word 'you' — rephrase your feedback.
studentStarter: There have been a few occasions recently where...
The issue seems to keep coming up with...
What I'd like us to focus on is...
studentFollowUp: Do you think it's easier to give criticism or to receive it? Why?

prompt: You're a tour guide. I'm the world's most difficult tourist. Let's go.
constraint: Student must respond to every complaint with a positive reframe.
studentPrompt: You are a tour guide and your tourist complains about everything. Respond to every complaint with something positive.
studentStarter: I understand it might seem that way, but actually...
The great thing about this is...
A lot of visitors feel that at first, but...
studentFollowUp: Have you ever been on a tour that you really enjoyed or really disliked?

prompt: I'm applying for a job I'm completely wrong for. You're the interviewer. Begin.
constraint: Student must use indirect rejection language — no flat refusals.
studentPrompt: You are interviewing someone who is completely wrong for the job. Let them down gently — no direct rejection.
studentStarter: We really appreciate you coming in today...
It's been great to meet you, and while...
We'll be in touch, though I should mention...
studentFollowUp: Have you ever had a job interview? How did it go?

prompt: You're the doctor. I'm the patient who googled everything and thinks I'm dying.
constraint: Student must ask clarifying questions before giving any diagnosis.
studentPrompt: You are a doctor and your patient has been googling their symptoms and is convinced something is seriously wrong. Ask questions before you respond to any of their worries.
studentStarter: Before we jump to any conclusions, can I ask...
How long have you been feeling this way?
Let's take this step by step — tell me...
studentFollowUp: Do you ever search for health information online? Do you trust what you find?

prompt: I'm your landlord. You've missed rent and I'm knocking on your door right now.
constraint: Student must negotiate a plan — not just apologise.
studentPrompt: Your landlord is at the door because you missed rent. Don't just apologise — explain your situation and suggest a plan.
studentStarter: I completely understand, and I want to be upfront with you...
I know this isn't ideal, but I have a plan...
If you can give me until..., I can guarantee...
studentFollowUp: Do you think it's easier to negotiate in your language or in English? Why?

prompt: You're a lawyer. I'm your client who is clearly guilty. Prepare our defence.
constraint: Student must never say the word 'guilty' or 'wrong'.
studentPrompt: You are a lawyer and your client is clearly guilty. Help prepare their defence without ever using the words 'guilty' or 'wrong'.
studentStarter: What we need to focus on is...
The key question the court will ask is...
Let's look at this from a different angle...
studentFollowUp: Do you think you would make a good lawyer? What skills would you need?

prompt: I'm a time traveller from 2150. Ask me anything about the future.
constraint: Student must ask follow-up questions — not just accept the first answer.
studentPrompt: Your teacher is a time traveller from 2150. Ask them about the future — and don't just accept their first answer. Dig deeper.
studentStarter: What's the biggest change since now?
Wait — can you tell me more about that?
So does that mean that...?
studentFollowUp: What year in the future would you most like to visit, and why?

prompt: You're a customer service agent. I want to return something I definitely broke myself.
constraint: Student must de-escalate without ever agreeing I'm right.
studentPrompt: You work in customer service. A customer wants to return something they clearly broke themselves. Calm the situation without agreeing that they are right.
studentStarter: I completely understand your frustration...
What I can do for you is...
Let me see what options we have available...
studentFollowUp: Have you ever had a frustrating experience with customer service?

prompt: Describe your perfect Saturday, in real time, as if it's happening right now.
constraint: Student speaks for 60 seconds without stopping. No questions allowed from me.
studentPrompt: Describe your perfect Saturday as if it's happening right now, in real time. Keep talking for as long as you can without stopping.
studentStarter: So right now I'm waking up slowly and...
It's about 10am and I'm just...
The first thing I do is...
studentFollowUp: What does your teacher's perfect Saturday look like?

prompt: Tell me the plot of a film you love — but make it sound terrible.
constraint: Student must use at least three words they wouldn't normally use.
studentPrompt: Tell your teacher the plot of a film you love — but make it sound as boring or terrible as possible. Try to use words you wouldn't normally use.
studentStarter: So basically it's about this incredibly dull man who...
The premise is frankly quite absurd...
For some inexplicable reason, millions of people love this film about...
studentFollowUp: What film does your teacher secretly love that they might be embarrassed about?

prompt: Explain what you do for a living to a very clever 8-year-old.
constraint: Student cannot use any jargon from their industry.
studentPrompt: Explain your job to a very clever 8-year-old. No jargon — use only simple, everyday words.
studentStarter: Okay so imagine that...
My job is basically like being a...
Every day I have to...
studentFollowUp: What job did you want to do when you were 8 years old?

prompt: Pitch me a product that doesn't exist yet. Sell it hard.
constraint: Student must mention a problem it solves, a price, and why I need it today.
studentPrompt: Invent a product that doesn't exist yet and sell it to your teacher. Mention the problem it solves, how much it costs, and why they need it right now.
studentPractice: Present simple for facts and features
studentStarter: Have you ever had the problem where...?
Introducing the only product that...
For just... you can have...
studentFollowUp: What product do you wish actually existed in real life?

prompt: You have one minute to convince me that your city is worth visiting.
constraint: Student cannot use the words 'beautiful', 'nice', or 'good'.
studentPrompt: Convince your teacher to visit your city in one minute. You cannot use the words 'beautiful', 'nice', or 'good' — find more interesting words.
studentStarter: The thing that makes my city unlike anywhere else is...
Most people don't realise that...
If you only do one thing there, you have to...
studentFollowUp: What city would your teacher recommend visiting, and why?

prompt: Walk me through your morning routine like a documentary narrator would.
constraint: Student must use at least two sequencing phrases (e.g. 'meanwhile', 'at this point').
studentPrompt: Describe your morning routine as if you are narrating a documentary about yourself. Use sequencing phrases to connect the events.
studentStarter: It begins, as it always does, with...
Meanwhile, across the city, our subject is...
At this point, a crucial decision must be made...
studentFollowUp: Is your teacher a morning person or a night person? How do they start their day?

prompt: Describe a rule you think is completely stupid and why it should change.
constraint: Student must give a specific alternative, not just complain.
studentPrompt: Tell your teacher about a rule you think is completely stupid. Explain why it's wrong and suggest a specific alternative — don't just complain.
studentStarter: The rule I can't stand is...
The reason it makes no sense is...
A much better approach would be to...
studentFollowUp: Does your teacher agree with you? Ask them if they'd change the same rule.

prompt: Tell me about a skill you wish you had — and what stopped you from getting it.
constraint: Student must speak for at least 45 seconds without me asking anything.
studentPrompt: Talk about a skill you wish you had and explain what got in the way of learning it. Try to keep speaking for at least 45 seconds without stopping.
studentPractice: Extending answers with reasons and examples
studentStarter: The skill I've always wished I had is...
I actually tried to learn it once, but...
If I could go back, I think I would have...
studentFollowUp: Is there a skill your teacher has that you admire? Ask them about it.

prompt: Describe the best meal you've ever eaten. Make me hungry.
constraint: Student must use sensory language: smell, texture, sound, appearance.
studentPrompt: Describe the best meal you have ever eaten in as much detail as possible. Use words for smell, texture, sound, and appearance to make your teacher hungry.
studentStarter: The smell hit me before I even sat down...
It looked almost too good to eat...
The texture was unlike anything I'd had before...
studentFollowUp: What is your teacher's most memorable meal? Ask them to describe it.

prompt: Explain the internet to someone who has never heard of it.
constraint: No analogies involving books, letters, or libraries.
studentPrompt: Explain the internet to someone who has never heard of it. You cannot use comparisons involving books, letters, or libraries — find other ways.
studentStarter: Imagine every person in the world could speak to each other at the same moment...
Think of it like an invisible city where...
It's a bit like having a conversation, except...
studentFollowUp: What would your teacher miss most if the internet disappeared tomorrow?

prompt: Talk me through the last time something really surprised you.
constraint: Student must include: what they expected, what happened, and how they felt.
studentPrompt: Tell your teacher about the last time something really surprised you. Include what you expected to happen, what actually happened, and how you felt.
studentStarter: I honestly thought it was going to be...
And then completely out of nowhere...
My first reaction was...
studentFollowUp: When was the last time something surprised your teacher? Ask them.

prompt: You ask the questions. I'll answer honestly. Interview me about my life.
constraint: Student must ask at least one follow-up to each of my answers.
studentPrompt: Interview your teacher about their life. After every answer they give, ask at least one follow-up question to find out more.
studentStarter: Where did you grow up?
What made you decide to become a teacher?
What's something most people don't know about you?
studentFollowUp: What was the most surprising thing you found out about your teacher?

prompt: Stop explaining. Ask me things instead. See how much you can find out in 3 minutes.
constraint: No yes/no questions allowed. Only open questions.
studentPrompt: Find out as much as you can about your teacher in 3 minutes using only open questions. No yes or no questions allowed.
studentPractice: Open questions with what, how, why, when, who
studentStarter: What's something you've changed your mind about recently?
How do you usually spend your evenings?
What's the most interesting place you've ever been?
studentFollowUp: Which answer surprised you the most?

prompt: You're the expert. Teach me something you know well in under 3 minutes.
constraint: Student must check I've understood at least twice during the explanation.
studentPrompt: Teach your teacher something you know well. You have 3 minutes. Check that they understand at least twice during your explanation.
studentStarter: So the first thing you need to know is...
Does that make sense so far?
The most important part is...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher if there's anything they've always wanted to learn how to do.

prompt: Give me advice. I'll tell you my problem — you tell me what to do.
constraint: Student must ask at least two questions before giving advice.
studentPrompt: Your teacher has a problem and needs advice. Before you give any advice, ask at least two questions to understand the situation better.
studentStarter: Before I say anything, can I ask...
How long has this been going on?
Have you already tried...?
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher if they find it easy or hard to ask for advice in real life.

prompt: You pick the topic. I'll talk about it — but you have to keep me going.
constraint: Student must ask a new question every time I slow down or stop.
studentPrompt: Choose a topic for your teacher to talk about. Your job is to keep them talking — ask a new question every time they slow down or stop.
studentStarter: Let's talk about...
Can you tell me more about that?
What do you mean exactly when you say...?
studentFollowUp: What topic would your teacher find easiest to talk about for a long time?

prompt: You decide what we talk about today. Completely your choice.
constraint: Student must introduce the topic, explain why they chose it, and ask me a question about it.
studentPrompt: Choose whatever topic you want to discuss today. Introduce it, explain why you chose it, and ask your teacher a question about it to start the conversation.
studentStarter: Today I'd like to talk about... because...
The reason I chose this is...
I'm curious what you think about...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher what topic they were hoping you'd choose.

prompt: I think social media has done more harm than good. Fight me.
constraint: Student must disagree and defend their view for at least 3 exchanges.
studentPrompt: Your teacher thinks social media has done more harm than good. Disagree and defend the opposite view for as long as you can.
studentStarter: I actually think that's too simple a view because...
The evidence suggests the opposite...
You could argue that, but consider...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher if they actually believe what they said or if they were just arguing for debate practice.

prompt: Working from home is better than working in an office. Convince me otherwise.
constraint: Student must give at least two concrete reasons, not just opinions.
studentPrompt: Your teacher thinks working from home is better than working in an office. Argue the opposite. Give at least two specific, concrete reasons.
studentStarter: The problem with that view is...
Studies actually show that...
One concrete example of why offices are better is...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher which they personally prefer and why.

prompt: Zoos should be banned. What do you think?
constraint: Student must give a clear position and defend it against my challenges.
studentPrompt: Give your opinion on whether zoos should be banned. Your teacher will challenge your view — defend it clearly.
studentStarter: My view is that...
I think zoos are important because...
Even if you argue that..., the fact is...
studentFollowUp: Have you ever been to a zoo? Did it change how you feel about them?

prompt: It's better to be honest than kind. Agree or disagree?
constraint: Student must use a personal example to support their view.
studentPrompt: Do you think it's better to be honest or kind? Give your view and back it up with a real example from your own life.
studentStarter: I think honesty is more important because...
In my experience, kindness actually...
There was a situation where I had to choose between the two and...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher about a time when they had to choose between being honest and being kind.

prompt: Money can buy happiness. Discuss.
constraint: Student must argue one side only — no 'it depends' answers.
studentPrompt: Argue one side of the debate: can money buy happiness? Pick a side and stick to it — no 'it depends' answers allowed.
studentStarter: I'm going to argue that money absolutely can...
The idea that money doesn't buy happiness is actually a myth because...
My position is clear: without money...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher what they would do with a million pounds if they had to spend it in a week.

prompt: School uniforms are a good idea. Do you agree?
constraint: Student must acknowledge the strongest argument against their view before continuing.
studentPrompt: Give your opinion on school uniforms. Before you make your main argument, acknowledge the strongest point against your view.
studentStarter: I can see why some people think..., but...
The strongest argument against my view is... however...
Admittedly..., but the bigger issue is...
studentFollowUp: Did your teacher have to wear a uniform at school? Ask them.

prompt: Learning a foreign language should be compulsory for everyone. Argue for or against.
constraint: Student must respond directly to my counterarguments — no ignoring them.
studentPrompt: Argue for or against making foreign language learning compulsory. When your teacher challenges you, respond directly to their point — don't ignore it.
studentStarter: That's a fair point, but it doesn't account for...
I'd push back on that because...
Even granting that..., it's still true that...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher how many languages they speak and whether they wish they'd learned more.

prompt: If you could only eat one cuisine for the rest of your life, what would it be and why?
constraint: Student must give at least three reasons and consider the downsides.
studentPrompt: If you could only eat one type of cuisine for the rest of your life, what would you choose? Give at least three reasons and think about the downsides too.
studentStarter: If I had to choose just one, it would definitely be...
The main reasons are...
The downside, I suppose, is that...
studentFollowUp: What would your teacher choose? Do you think you could ever agree?

prompt: You wake up tomorrow and everyone on earth can read minds. What changes?
constraint: Student must think about both positive and negative consequences.
studentPrompt: Imagine you wake up and everyone on earth can suddenly read minds. What changes? Think about both the good and the bad consequences.
studentStarter: On one hand, it would mean...
The most obvious problem would be...
I think society would change because...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher whose mind they would most want to read.

prompt: If you could live in any decade in history — but not your own — when would you choose?
constraint: Student must consider both the advantages and what they'd miss about today.
studentPrompt: If you could live in any other decade in history, which would you pick? Say what appeals to you and what you'd miss about living today.
studentPractice: Talking about history and time periods
studentStarter: I think I'd choose the... because...
What appeals to me about that era is...
The thing I'd miss most about today is...
studentFollowUp: Which decade would your teacher choose? Ask them.

prompt: If you had to give up one of your five senses, which would it be and why?
constraint: Student must explain the impact on their daily life specifically.
studentPrompt: If you had to give up one of your five senses, which would it be? Explain specifically how it would affect your everyday life.
studentStarter: As awful as it sounds, I think I'd give up...
The reason I'd choose that one is...
In terms of my daily life, the main impact would be...
studentFollowUp: Which sense does your teacher think they'd miss the most? Ask them.

prompt: You've been given £1 million but must spend it all in 48 hours. What do you do?
constraint: Student must have a plan — not just a list of things to buy.
studentPrompt: You have £1 million and exactly 48 hours to spend it all. Tell your teacher your plan — not just a shopping list, but an actual strategy.
studentStarter: The first thing I'd do is...
My strategy would be to...
By the end of the 48 hours, I'd have...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher what they'd do — and whether their plan is more or less sensible than yours.

prompt: If animals could talk, which species would have the most interesting things to say?
constraint: Student must give reasons and imagine specific things the animal might say.
studentPrompt: If animals could talk, which species would have the most interesting things to say? Give your reasons and imagine some specific things they might tell us.
studentStarter: I think it would be... because...
They might say something like...
What would be fascinating is if they told us...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher which animal they think would be the most annoying to talk to.

prompt: If you could only keep three apps on your phone, which would you choose and why?
constraint: Student must justify each choice and explain what they'd sacrifice.
studentPrompt: You can only keep three apps on your phone. Which do you choose? Justify each one and explain what you'd be giving up.
studentStarter: The first one I'd keep is... because...
As much as I'd miss it, I'd delete...
The hardest one to give up would be...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher which three apps they'd keep and whether they could survive without social media.

prompt: If you could swap lives with someone for a week, who would it be?
constraint: Student must explain what they'd learn from the experience.
studentPrompt: If you could swap lives with anyone for a week, who would you choose? Explain what you'd want to experience and what you think you'd learn.
studentStarter: I'd choose to swap with... because...
What I'd most want to experience is...
I think I'd learn a lot about...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher whose life they'd swap with — and whether they'd want to swap back after a week.

prompt: Tell me about your weekend — but every sentence must start with a different word.
constraint: Student cannot repeat a sentence-opening word. I'll be watching.
studentPrompt: Tell your teacher about your weekend, but every sentence must start with a different word. No repeating sentence openers.
studentStarter: Saturday started with...
Later, I decided to...
Honestly, the best part was...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher to try the same challenge — talk about their weekend with no repeated sentence starters.

prompt: Talk about anything you want — but you cannot use the verb 'to be' in any form.
constraint: No 'is', 'are', 'was', 'were', 'been', 'am'. Find alternatives.
studentPrompt: Talk about anything you like, but you cannot use any form of the verb 'to be': no is, are, was, were, am, or been. Find other ways to express your ideas.
studentStarter: Something I find really interesting lately...
My weekend went quite well because...
The thing I enjoy most right now...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher to spot any moments where you accidentally used 'to be' — and try to correct them together.

prompt: Describe your hometown — but only using questions.
constraint: Every sentence must be a question. Statements are not allowed.
studentPrompt: Describe your hometown using only questions. You cannot make any statements — every sentence must be a question.
studentStarter: Have you ever seen a city where...?
Can you imagine a place that has...?
What would you think of somewhere that...?
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher to guess what your hometown is like based only on the questions you asked.

prompt: Tell me a story — but every verb must be in the passive voice.
constraint: Active verbs are not allowed. Rephrase everything into passive structures.
studentPrompt: Tell your teacher a short story using only passive verb structures. No active verbs — rephrase everything.
studentStarter: It was discovered that...
The decision had been made to...
Eventually, it was found that...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher which passive structure they think sounds the most natural in English.

prompt: Have a conversation with me — but you must respond to everything I say with a question.
constraint: You may not make statements. Every single turn must be a question.
studentPrompt: Respond to everything your teacher says with a question. You cannot make any statements — every single turn must be a question.
studentStarter: Really? Why do you think that?
What made you feel that way?
And what happened after that?
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher which of your questions was the most interesting or unexpected.

prompt: Tell me about something important to you — but speak only in the future tense.
constraint: No past or present tense. Everything must be future-focused.
studentPrompt: Talk about something important to you using only the future tense. No past or present — everything must be future-focused.
studentPractice: Future tenses: will, going to, future continuous
studentStarter: This will always matter to me because...
In the future, I'm going to...
I think it will continue to...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher what they think your future looks like based on what you just said.

prompt: Start a story with: 'Nobody believed her, but she had seen it with her own eyes.'
constraint: Student must continue for at least 5 sentences before I can ask anything.
studentPrompt: Continue the story that starts with: 'Nobody believed her, but she had seen it with her own eyes.' Keep going for at least 5 sentences before stopping.
studentStarter: She had been walking home when...
The strange thing was...
What nobody knew was that...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher what they think happened next — let them continue the story.

prompt: Tell me a story that starts with an ending and works backwards.
constraint: Student must begin at the conclusion and reveal how we got there, step by step.
studentPrompt: Tell your teacher a story that starts at the ending and works backwards. Begin with the conclusion, then reveal how it happened step by step.
studentStarter: So that's how I ended up...
Three days earlier, none of this had happened yet...
It had all started when...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher if they prefer stories told forwards or backwards — and why.

prompt: Create a character who is the opposite of you in every way. Describe their day.
constraint: Student must describe at least 4 ways the character differs from themselves.
studentPrompt: Invent a character who is the complete opposite of you in every way. Describe at least four differences and walk your teacher through their typical day.
studentStarter: Unlike me, this person...
While I prefer..., they always...
Their morning looks nothing like mine — they...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher which character they'd rather spend a day with — you or your opposite.

prompt: Tell me the most embarrassing story you're willing to share.
constraint: Student must tell it as a proper story — with a beginning, middle, and end.
studentPrompt: Tell your teacher an embarrassing story from your life. Tell it properly — with a beginning, middle, and end.
studentStarter: So this happened a few years ago when...
To give you some background...
The most embarrassing part was when...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher to share their most embarrassing story in return.

prompt: We are both characters in the same story. You decide the setting. Begin.
constraint: Student must establish the setting, the situation, and both characters within the first 3 sentences.
studentPrompt: You and your teacher are both characters in a story. You decide the setting. In your first three sentences, establish where you are, what the situation is, and who your characters are.
studentStarter: It's 1943, and we're both hiding in...
The year is 2087. We've just landed on...
It's a small town in the middle of nowhere, and the two of us have just...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher what they think should happen next in the story.

prompt: Retell a fairy tale but set it in the modern world.
constraint: Student must keep the key plot points but update every detail to today.
studentPrompt: Retell a classic fairy tale but set it in the modern world. Keep the key plot points but update every detail — characters, settings, and situations — to today.
studentStarter: So this is basically Cinderella, but she's a...
Once upon a time in a city very much like this one...
Think of the Big Bad Wolf as a...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher to guess which fairy tale you were retelling.

prompt: Tell me a true story from your life in exactly 10 sentences.
constraint: Not 9. Not 11. Exactly 10.
studentPrompt: Tell your teacher a true story from your life in exactly 10 sentences. Not 9, not 11 — exactly 10.
studentStarter: Sentence 1: set the scene. Sentence 2: introduce the problem. Then take it from there.
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher to count your sentences and check you hit exactly 10.

prompt: You need to cancel plans with me at the last minute. Do it.
constraint: Student must apologise sincerely, give a reason, and offer an alternative.
studentPrompt: Cancel your plans with your teacher at the last minute. Apologise sincerely, give a reason, and suggest an alternative.
studentStarter: I'm so sorry to do this, but...
I feel terrible about this — something has come up...
Would it be okay if we...instead?
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher how they usually react when someone cancels on them.

prompt: You've been overcharged at a restaurant. Speak to the manager.
constraint: Student must be polite but firm. No aggressive language. Reach a solution.
studentPrompt: You've been overcharged at a restaurant and need to speak to the manager. Be polite but firm, and try to reach a solution.
studentStarter: Excuse me, I think there might be a mistake on my bill...
I don't want to cause any trouble, but...
Could we look at this together and...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher if they've ever had to complain at a restaurant — and whether it worked.

prompt: You're at a party and don't know anyone. I'm standing alone. Start a conversation.
constraint: Student must find one genuine point of connection before the conversation ends.
studentPrompt: You're at a party where you don't know anyone. Start a conversation with your teacher, who is standing alone. Try to find something you have in common.
studentPractice: Small talk and finding common ground
studentStarter: Hi — do you know many people here?
Great party, right? How do you know the host?
I love your... Where did you get it?
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher what their go-to opening line is when they meet someone new.

prompt: Leave me a voicemail explaining why you'll be late to an important meeting.
constraint: Student must be clear, brief, and give an accurate ETA.
studentPrompt: Leave a voicemail for your teacher explaining why you'll be late to an important meeting. Be clear and brief, and give a realistic arrival time.
studentStarter: Hi, it's... I'm calling because...
I wanted to let you know as soon as possible that...
I should be there by... at the latest.
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher whether they prefer voicemails, texts, or calls when someone is running late.

prompt: You've found an amazing flat to rent but it's slightly over budget. Negotiate.
constraint: Student must offer something concrete in exchange — not just ask for a discount.
studentPrompt: You've found a great flat to rent but it's slightly over your budget. Negotiate with the landlord — offer something concrete, don't just ask for a discount.
studentStarter: I'm really interested in the flat, but I was hoping we could discuss the price...
What if I offered to...?
Would you consider... if I...?
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher if they've ever had to negotiate something in real life — and how it went.

prompt: A colleague takes credit for your idea in a meeting. What do you do — right now?
constraint: Student must speak up in the moment, politely but clearly.
studentPrompt: Your teacher has just taken credit for your idea in a meeting. Speak up right now, in the moment — politely but clearly.
studentStarter: Actually, I think I should mention that...
I'm glad you brought that up — it came from our conversation where I suggested...
Just to add some context to that idea...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher whether they think it's easy or hard to speak up in meetings.

prompt: You're returning a product without a receipt. Make your case.
constraint: Student cannot lie or invent details. Build a polite, honest argument.
studentPrompt: You want to return a product but you don't have a receipt. Make your case honestly — no lies or invented details.
studentStarter: I understand this might be difficult without the receipt, but...
I bought it about... ago and the problem is...
Is there anything we can do in this situation?
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher what their store return policy pet peeve is.

prompt: Give a 90-second speech at your friend's birthday dinner. You forgot to prepare.
constraint: Student must open warmly, tell one specific memory, and close with a toast.
studentPrompt: Give an unplanned 90-second speech at your friend's birthday dinner. Open warmly, share one specific memory about them, and end with a toast.
studentStarter: Right, okay, so... I wasn't prepared for this, but...
I just want to say a few words about...
I'll never forget the time when...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher if they're good at improvised speeches — or do they need to prepare?

prompt: Ask a neighbour to turn down their music — for the third time.
constraint: Student must be assertive without being aggressive. Mention consequences.
studentPrompt: Ask your neighbour to turn down their music — this is the third time you've had to ask. Be assertive without being aggressive, and mention what will happen if it continues.
studentStarter: Look, I'm sorry to keep coming back about this, but...
I've mentioned it a couple of times now and...
If this continues, I'm going to have to...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher how they deal with noisy neighbours — or difficult situations with people they live near.

prompt: You've accidentally sent an email to the wrong person at work. Handle the fallout.
constraint: Student must assess how bad it is, apologise, and propose a fix.
studentPrompt: You sent a work email to the wrong person by mistake. Handle the situation — assess how serious it is, apologise, and propose a fix.
studentStarter: I need to be upfront with you — I made a mistake...
I sent an email that was meant for... to... by accident.
Here's what I'd like to do to fix this...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher if they've ever sent an email to the wrong person — what happened?

prompt: What's one thing people in your country believe that you personally think is wrong?
constraint: Student must separate their opinion from the cultural belief clearly.
studentPrompt: Share one thing that people in your country commonly believe that you personally disagree with. Make it clear what the general belief is and what your own view is.
studentPractice: Distinguishing personal views from cultural norms
studentStarter: A lot of people where I'm from believe that..., but personally I think...
It's widely accepted in my country that..., though I'd argue...
Most people around me would say..., whereas I feel...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher if they've ever changed their mind about a cultural belief they grew up with.

prompt: Is it possible to be truly happy and completely bored at the same time? Argue it.
constraint: Student must give an example from real life, not just theory.
studentPrompt: Can a person be truly happy and completely bored at the same time? Argue your position and use a real life example to back it up.
studentStarter: I actually think these two can coexist because...
There was a time in my life when...
The distinction I'd make is between...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher if they've ever felt happy and bored at the same time.

prompt: What's something almost everyone does that you find quietly strange?
constraint: Student must describe it without judgement — just curiosity.
studentPrompt: What's something that almost everyone does that you find a little strange? Describe it with curiosity, not judgement.
studentStarter: This might sound odd, but I've always found it a bit strange that...
I'm not judging at all, but...
Something I notice a lot that I don't quite understand is...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher if they do the thing you described — and whether they've ever thought about why.

prompt: Name one thing that's considered rude in your culture but normal elsewhere.
constraint: Student must explain the logic behind the rule, not just state it.
studentPrompt: Tell your teacher about something considered rude in your culture that's perfectly normal somewhere else. Explain the logic behind the rule — don't just state it.
studentStarter: In my culture, it's considered quite rude to...
The reason behind it is...
What's interesting is that in other countries...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher if they've ever accidentally broken a cultural rule in another country.

prompt: Would you rather know everything that's true, or believe everything that makes you happy?
constraint: Student must give their answer, then steelman the opposite view.
studentPrompt: Would you rather know everything that's true, or believe everything that makes you happy? Give your honest answer — then make the strongest possible case for the opposite view.
studentStarter: My honest answer is...
But if I'm being fair to the other side...
The strongest argument against my view is probably...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher which they would choose — and whether they'd answer differently in private.

prompt: What does success mean to you — and has that definition ever changed?
constraint: Student must give a before and after if it has changed, or explain why it hasn't.
studentPrompt: What does success mean to you? If your definition has changed over time, describe the before and after. If it hasn't, explain why it's stayed the same.
studentStarter: For me, success means...
When I was younger, I used to think success was...
What changed my perspective was...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher how their definition of success has changed since they were a student.

prompt: Is there a piece of advice you received that turned out to be completely wrong?
constraint: Student must explain who gave it, why they believed it, and when it broke down.
studentPrompt: Tell your teacher about advice you once received that turned out to be completely wrong. Say who gave it, why you believed it at the time, and when you realised it wasn't true.
studentStarter: Someone I trusted once told me that...
At the time I completely believed it because...
The moment I realised it was wrong was when...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher for the best piece of advice they've ever received.

prompt: What's something you're better at than most people, but never talk about?
constraint: Student must explain how they got good at it.
studentPrompt: Share something you're genuinely better at than most people but rarely mention. Explain how you developed this skill or ability.
studentStarter: This might sound like I'm showing off, but...
Something I've never really mentioned is that I'm actually quite good at...
I got good at it because...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher what hidden skill or talent they have that most people don't know about.

prompt: If you could ask one question and get a guaranteed honest answer from anyone, what would it be?
constraint: Student must explain what they'd do with the answer.
studentPrompt: If you could ask one question and get a guaranteed honest answer from anyone in the world, what would you ask? Explain what you'd do with the answer.
studentStarter: The question I'd ask is...
The reason I'd choose that question is...
And once I had the answer, I would...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher what question they would ask — and whether they'd really want to know the answer.

prompt: What's the nicest thing a stranger has ever done for you?
constraint: Student must describe the stranger in detail and explain why it stuck with them.
studentPrompt: Tell your teacher about the nicest thing a stranger has ever done for you. Describe the stranger, what they did, and explain why the memory has stayed with you.
studentStarter: This happened a few years ago when...
The stranger was...
What made it stick with me was...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher about a time they did something kind for a stranger.

prompt: I'll name a topic. You speak about it for 30 seconds without stopping. Ready? Childhood.
constraint: No topic is off-limits. No pausing for more than 2 seconds or I call it.
studentPrompt: Your teacher will name a topic. Speak about it for 30 seconds without stopping. No pausing for more than 2 seconds.
studentPractice: Keeping talk going with fillers and examples
studentStarter: The first thing that comes to mind is...
When I think about this topic, I immediately think of...
This reminds me of...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher to try the same topic and see if they can last 30 seconds without pausing.

prompt: Convince me to watch the last thing you watched on TV or streaming.
constraint: Student has 45 seconds. No plot spoilers. Sell the feeling, not the facts.
studentPrompt: Convince your teacher to watch the last thing you watched on TV or streaming. You have 45 seconds. No spoilers — sell the feeling, not the plot.
studentStarter: You need to watch this because...
It's the kind of show that makes you feel...
I can't explain the plot without spoiling it, but imagine...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher what the last thing they watched was — and whether they'd recommend it.

prompt: Three things you wish you'd known at age 18. Go.
constraint: Student must explain each one briefly — not just list them.
studentPrompt: Name three things you wish you'd known at age 18. Explain each one briefly — don't just list them.
studentStarter: The first thing is... and the reason is...
I wish I'd known that...
Looking back, I really wish someone had told me...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher what the most important thing they'd tell their 18-year-old self is.

prompt: What's the most interesting thing that's happened to you this week? One minute.
constraint: Student cannot say 'nothing happened'. Find something — anything.
studentPrompt: Tell your teacher the most interesting thing that happened to you this week. You cannot say 'nothing happened' — find something, however small.
studentStarter: Okay so it's not the most dramatic thing, but...
Something small but kind of interesting happened when...
Actually, now I think about it...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher what the most interesting thing that happened to them this week was.

prompt: Give me your hot take on something trivial. Something you have strong feelings about.
constraint: Student must be specific. No generic opinions.
studentPrompt: Give your teacher your strongest opinion on something completely trivial. Be specific — no vague or generic opinions.
studentStarter: My controversial opinion is...
I feel very strongly that...
Nobody talks about this enough, but...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher for their most unpopular trivial opinion.

prompt: Two truths and a lie. Go.
constraint: I will ask follow-up questions to detect the lie. Student must defend all three.
studentPrompt: Tell your teacher two true things about yourself and one lie. Your teacher will ask follow-up questions — defend all three statements convincingly.
studentStarter: Okay, here are my three statements...
The first one is...
All three are completely true. Obviously.
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher to try two truths and a lie — and see if you can spot the lie.

prompt: Name three things in this room and find something they have in common.
constraint: Student has 60 seconds. The connection cannot be 'they're all objects'.
studentPrompt: Look around the room and pick three objects. Find something they have in common. You have 60 seconds — and the connection can't be 'they're all objects'.
studentStarter: Okay so I'm choosing...
What they have in common is...
I know that sounds strange, but think about it...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher to pick three different objects and try to find a more interesting connection than yours.

prompt: Teach me one word from your language that has no English equivalent.
constraint: Student must explain when you'd use it, with a real example.
studentPrompt: Teach your teacher one word from your language that has no direct English translation. Explain what it means, when you'd use it, and give a real example.
studentStarter: The word is... and there's really no English equivalent.
You'd use it when...
For example, imagine you're in a situation where...
studentFollowUp: Ask your teacher if they know any words from other languages that English doesn't have.
